source: CNBC Sharpe Angle — SPAC market structure, PIPE financing dynamics, retail investor trends
summary: High-quality 'serious' SPACs backed by sophisticated sponsors will survive and return; unscrupulous SPACs will not come back
summary_extended: Baker argues that the SPAC market should be understood as three distinct categories: unserious SPACs (low-quality sponsors), unscrupulous SPACs (predatory structures, heavily concentrated in EV), and serious SPACs backed by sophisticated, high-quality sponsors. He believes the first two categories will not return after the 2020-2021 boom-bust cycle, while the third category — serious SPACs with aligned fee structures and credible operators — will persist and become a durable part of the capital markets landscape. Baker expects the market to normalize at a level above pre-2020 baselines but well below the peak frenzy, with structural improvements such as deferred and performance-aligned fees becoming standard practice. The investment implication is not a specific equity but rather a structural view: investors should selectively engage with SPACs only from high-quality sponsors and avoid the broader SPAC market as a category, as the predatory and unserious segments are unlikely to recover.
detail: Baker distinguishes three SPAC categories: unserious, unscrupulous (predatory, concentrated in EV), and serious (high-quality sponsors, sophisticated operators). He expects the serious category to persist and the market to normalize at a higher level than 2019 but below the 2020-2021 peak. Structural improvements like deferred/aligned fees will become standard.
confidence_evidence: Baker states 'that third group will be here to stay' and provides structural reasoning; he has been an active participant in this category
quote_fragment: serious high quality SPAC sponsors will be here to stay
